pattern: Repeated subtraction of the smaller value from the larger is exactly the arithmetic underneath the Euclidean algorithm, and counting how many times it fires is the same as counting how many whole multiples get consumed at each step.
hints: Simulating one subtraction at a time is already fast enough given how small the inputs are, so a direct loop works.
Whenever one value is much bigger than the other, many identical subtractions happen in a row before the roles swap.
That run of identical subtractions can be collapsed into a single division-and-remainder step instead of looping one-by-one.

edge_inputs.description: one value is a clean multiple of the other
smaller value at the minimum useful bound
roles of the previous case swapped
both values at the maximum bound and equal
values that are coprime, forcing many alternating subtractions

stress.description: random pairs across the full bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    num1 = rng.randint(0, hi)
    num2 = rng.randint(0, hi)
    return [num1, num2]
